prevalence.trend: stable due to high H. pylori endemicity

riskFactors: H. pylori infection (>70% prevalence in India)
NSAID use (common in elderly and chronic pain management)
Helicobacter pylori reinfection in endemic areas
vulnerablePopulations: Low socioeconomic groups in rural areas
Individuals with family history of PUD
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: ISG guidelines: Clinical symptoms (epigastric pain), endoscopy with biopsy for confirmation, H. pylori testing (urea breath test or stool antigen)

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: limited H. pylori screening (primarily in urban tertiary centers)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: 65% with PPI and antibiotic therapy adherence

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: ISG criteria: Clinical diagnosis common in primary care, endoscopy reserved for complications like bleeding or perforation
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: PPIs (e.g., pantoprazole) + triple therapy for H. pylori (amoxicillin, clarithromycin, PPI for 14 days), lifestyle changes (reduce spicy food intake), Ayurvedic options (e.g., Yashtimadhu - licorice for gastroprotection)
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Very high H. pylori prevalence (>70%), spicy diet culture exacerbating symptoms, limited endoscopy access in rural areas, NSAID overuse in self-medication
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Affordable generic PPIs and antibiotics, integration of Ayurvedic gastroprotectives, ISG guidelines promoting evidence-based care
